narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 35, you've put together a respectable profile for law school, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 40.4%—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants with stronger projected odds of bar success. The decision hinged on just a few key factors from your background, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the typical 5.53 average—emerging as the single biggest influence, strongly signaling to the model a heightened chance of bar struggles. Remarkably close behind, your undergraduate GPA pushed in the opposite direction, acting as a major counterbalance by suggesting solid academic potential compared to the average of 3.21. Your LSAT score provided additional positive support, nudging the prediction toward success despite being slightly under the usual 36.70 mark. Overall, while your GPA and LSAT helped mitigate concerns, they weren't quite enough to offset the red flag from your lower Year 3 performance, which carried the most weight in tipping the scales toward failure risk. This pattern sets your profile apart from typical admitted students, who often show consistently higher decile rankings across years alongside similar test scores. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your full-time commitment and background aligning with the norm. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year grades, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also target schools with more holistic reviews or prep intensively for the LSAT to exceed averages. Exploring post-baccalaureate options or bar prep-focused programs could further demonstrate your readiness. With targeted improvements, your strong GPA foundation positions you well for another shot at success.
